SECTION 1: PROJECT OVERVIEW
================================================================================

Q1. Tell me about this project in 2-3 sentences.
----------------------------------------------------
A: BudgetMart is a real-time grocery price comparison web app built for Indian
consumers. Users search for a product like "Amul Milk" and instantly see prices
from multiple sellers fetched live via Google Shopping through SerpAPI. I built
the full stack — from a vanilla JS frontend to a Flask backend deployed serverless
on Vercel — to keep it lightweight and free to host.

Q2. What problem does BudgetMart solve?
-----------------------------------------
A: In India, grocery prices can vary significantly across platforms like BigBasket,
Blinkit, Swiggy Instamart, and JioMart. Customers waste time opening 3-4 apps
just to find the cheapest option. BudgetMart aggregates all of that into one
search, saving time and money. It also adds location-awareness so you get results
relevant to your city.

Q3. What was your motivation for building it?
----------------------------------------------
A: I wanted to build something that had real-world utility while practicing full-
stack development. I specifically chose not to use a big framework like React or
Django to force myself to understand core web mechanics — how HTTP requests work,
how the DOM is manipulated, how APIs are consumed. I also wanted hands-on
experience with third-party API integration and cloud deployment.

Q4. What is the tech stack and why did you choose each piece?
--------------------------------------------------------------
A:
  - Vanilla JS (no React/Vue): Wanted to keep the frontend lightweight and avoid
    build toolchains. Since the app is essentially a search UI, a framework would
    have been overkill.

  - Flask (not Django/FastAPI): Flask is minimal and easy to understand. For an
    app with only 3 routes, Django's "batteries included" would be unnecessary.
    FastAPI would make sense if I needed async routes, but Flask + synchronous
    SerpAPI calls was simpler.

  - SerpAPI (not direct scraping): Scraping Google Shopping directly would be
    unreliable — Google actively blocks scrapers. SerpAPI handles CAPTCHA,
    proxy rotation, and DOM parsing as a managed service.

  - Vercel (not Heroku/Render): Vercel has a generous free tier with no credit
    card required, built-in GitHub integration, and fast global CDN. It supports
    Python serverless functions natively.

Q5. Walk me through the user flow end to end.
----------------------------------------------
A: The user opens the frontend (static HTML/CSS/JS served from Vercel CDN).
They type a product name and optional location, then click Search. JavaScript
captures the form submit event, shows a loading spinner, and calls the Flask
backend via fetch(). Flask validates the query, checks the in-memory cache, and
if it's a cache miss, calls SerpAPI with the Google Shopping engine. SerpAPI
returns up to 12 product results with prices, images, and platform names. Flask
normalizes this data, caches it for 5 minutes, and returns JSON. The frontend
receives the JSON, sorts results by price (in-stock items first), generates HTML
cards dynamically, and injects them into the DOM. The user sees a grid of product
cards with prices, and can click "Visit Store" to go to the actual product page.

SECTION 2: FRONTEND QUESTIONS
================================================================================

Q6. Why did you choose Vanilla JS over React or Vue?
------------------------------------------------------
A: The app has very simple state: a search query, a list of results, and a sort
order. React's component model and state management (useState, useEffect, props)
would add complexity with no real benefit here. Vanilla JS with one global
currentResults array was cleaner. I also didn't need a virtual DOM — I render
cards by building innerHTML strings and appending to the DOM once, which is
efficient for this use case.

Q7. How does the frontend know whether to use localhost or the production URL?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A: In script.js, I check window.location.hostname:
    const API_URL = (window.location.hostname === '127.0.0.1' ||
                     window.location.hostname === 'localhost')
        ? 'http://127.0.0.1:5000'
        : 'https://budgetmart-backend.vercel.app';

  This means the same unmodified file works in both environments without any
  build step or environment variable injection.

Q8. Explain how you dynamically render product cards.
------------------------------------------------------
A: After receiving the JSON array from the backend, I call renderResults().
Inside, I:
  1. Clear the resultsGrid div (innerHTML = '')
  2. Sort the results array based on the current dropdown value
  3. Find the minimum price among in-stock items
  4. Loop through each item and call document.createElement('div')
  5. Set className, style.animationDelay, and innerHTML on the card
  6. Call resultsGrid.appendChild(card)

The innerHTML is a template literal string that conditionally includes the
image, badges, price, and delivery info based on item properties.

Q9. How do you highlight the best price?
-----------------------------------------
A: In renderResults(), after sorting, I compute:
    const inStockItems = sortedResults.filter(r => r.stock && r.price !== null);
    const lowestPrice = Math.min(...inStockItems.map(item => item.price));

  Then for each card, I check: isBestValue = (item.stock && item.price === lowestPrice)
  If true, I add the 'best-value' CSS class to the card.
  The CSS for .best-value adds a green border, a "BEST PRICE" badge overlay,
  and a glow effect.

Q10. How does the platform badge color generation work?
--------------------------------------------------------
A: I didn't want to hardcode colors per platform because new platforms could
appear in Google Shopping results at any time. Instead, I generate a stable
color from the platform name string using a hash:
    let hash = 0;
    for (let i = 0; i < item.platform.length; i++) {
        hash = item.platform.charCodeAt(i) + ((hash << 5) - hash);
    }
    const color = `hsl(${Math.abs(hash) % 360}, 70%, 50%)`;

  This produces a unique, consistent HSL color for every platform name.
  "BigBasket" always maps to the same hue. It uses JavaScript's bitwise shift.

Q11. How does the sorting work?
---------------------------------
A: Two modes:

  "price_asc" (default): Sorts by price ascending, but in-stock items always
  appear before out-of-stock items. If both are in-stock or both OOS, sort by
  price numerically (OOS items use Infinity as their price so they go last).

  "availability": Just separates in-stock vs OOS (in-stock first), otherwise
  maintains the original order from SerpAPI.

  The sort comparator returns: -1 (a before b), 1 (b before a), or 0 (equal).

Q12. Explain the staggered card animation.
-------------------------------------------
A: Each card gets an inline style: animationDelay = `${index * 0.1}s`
  This means card 0 animates at 0s, card 1 at 0.1s, card 2 at 0.2s, etc.
  The CSS class 'card' has a @keyframes 'slideUp' animation that moves the card
  from translateY(20px) with opacity 0 to normal position with opacity 1.
  The combined effect is a cascading wave as cards appear one after another.

Q13. How do you handle errors in the frontend?
------------------------------------------------
A: The fetchResults function has a try/catch. If response.ok is false,
I read the JSON body to get the error message, then throw new Error(errorData.error).
In the catch block, I hide the loader, set errorText.textContent to the error
message, and show the error div. This handles: network failures, 400 bad request,
404 not found, and 500 server errors.

Q14. What is glassmorphism and how did you implement it?
---------------------------------------------------------
A: Glassmorphism is a design trend that creates "frosted glass" UI panels.
Key CSS properties:
  background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.05);   /* very transparent white */
  backdrop-filter: blur(20px);             /* blurs what's behind the panel */
  border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.1); /* faint border */
  border-radius: 16px;                     /* rounded corners */

The backdrop-filter blur creates the frosted glass effect on the dark background
with animated gradient blobs behind it.

Q15. Walk me through the Flask backend architecture.
------------------------------------------------------
A: It's organized as a Flask app with a services/ package:
  - index.py: App entry point, route definitions, CORS, caching config
  - services/serp_service.py: Core business logic (API call + data parsing)
  - services/normalizer.py: Utility functions for data normalization
  - services/bigbasket_service.py, blinkit_service.py, instamart_service.py:
    Mock async services (scaffolding for future direct platform scraping)

This follows a separation of concerns pattern — routes don't contain business
logic, they delegate to service functions.

Q16. What is Flask-CORS and why do you need it?
------------------------------------------------
A: CORS = Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. Browsers enforce a Same-Origin Policy
that blocks JavaScript on one domain from making fetch requests to a different
domain. My frontend (budgetmart.vercel.app) makes requests to the backend
(budgetmart-backend.vercel.app) — different subdomains = different origins.

Flask-CORS adds the necessary response headers:
  Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
  Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, OPTIONS

Without it, the browser would refuse the request with a CORS error before even
sending it to Flask.

Q17. Explain Flask-Caching and how it's configured.
-----------------------------------------------------
A: Flask-Caching wraps Flask's view functions with a cache layer.
Configuration:
  cache = Cache(app, config={
      'CACHE_TYPE': 'SimpleCache',      # RAM-based dict, no external service
      'CACHE_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT': 300      # 5-minute TTL
  })

The @cache.cached(query_string=True) decorator on the /search route means:
  - First call: execute the function, store result in dict with URL as key
  - Subsequent calls within 5 min: return stored result, skip function body

SimpleCache is a thread-safe Python dict. It's suitable for development and
light production, but not for multi-process deployments (Vercel serverless).

Q18. Why did you use SimpleCache instead of Redis?
---------------------------------------------------
A: Simplicity and cost. SimpleCache requires zero setup — it's just an in-memory
Python dict. Redis would require running a separate Redis server (adds cost and
complexity). For a personal project with low traffic, SimpleCache is perfectly
fine. However, I'm aware of the limitation: on Vercel's serverless architecture,
each cold-start creates a new Python process, so the cache is wiped. For
production at scale, I'd swap to Upstash Redis (serverless Redis with a free tier).

Q19. How does the /search route handle missing parameters?
-----------------------------------------------------------
A: After reading the query param:
    query = request.args.get("product", "").strip()
    if not query:
        return jsonify({"error": "Please provide a search product"}), 400

This returns an HTTP 400 Bad Request with a JSON error message. The .strip()
handles cases where the user only submitted whitespace.

Q20. Explain the data returned by SerpAPI and how you parse it.
----------------------------------------------------------------
A: SerpAPI returns a JSON dict with a "shopping_results" key containing a list
of products. Each product item has fields like:
  - title: Product name string
  - extracted_price: Float (cleaned from price string like "₹45.00")
  - source: Seller/platform name string
  - link: Direct product URL
  - thumbnail: Product image URL
  - delivery: Delivery info string (e.g., "Free delivery")

My parsing loop (in serp_service.py):
  1. Takes shopping_results[:12] (top 12 only)
  2. Gets extracted_price (already a float, no parsing needed)
  3. Uses item.get("link") or item.get("product_link") or "#" for URL
     (fallback chain in case primary link is missing)
  4. Runs two regex passes on title to extract quantity
  5. Sets stock = True if price is truthy (not None, not 0)
  6. Builds normalized dict and appends to parsed_data list

Q21. Why do you limit to 12 results?
--------------------------------------
A: To balance completeness vs. UX performance. The top 12 results from Google
Shopping are almost always from the most relevant major retailers. Beyond 12,
you start getting duplicates or less relevant sellers. Also, the frontend renders
cards in a grid — 12 cards fill the screen well without overwhelming the user.
It also reduces SerpAPI response size and frontend rendering time.

Q22. Explain the regex used for quantity extraction.
-----------------------------------------------------
A: Two patterns are used on the product title string:

Pattern 1 (pack/set):
  r'((?:pack|set)\s+of\s+\d+)'  with re.IGNORECASE
  (?:pack|set) = non-capturing group matching "pack" or "set"
  \s+ = one or more whitespace characters
  of = literal "of"
  \s+ = whitespace
  \d+ = one or more digits
  Example matches: "Pack of 6", "Set of 12", "pack of 2"

Pattern 2 (weight/volume/units):
  r'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:ml|l|litre|litres|g|kg|gm|piece|pieces|pc|pcs|unit|units))\b'
  \d+ = digits (mandatory)
  (?:\.\d+)? = optional decimal part
  \s* = optional space between number and unit
  (?:ml|l|...) = one of the unit options
  \b = word boundary (prevents "mg" matching inside longer words)
  Example matches: "500ml", "1 kg", "250 gm", "6 pieces", "12 pcs"

Results are joined: quantity_str = " | ".join(found_qty)

Q23. What happens if SerpAPI fails or the key is invalid?
----------------------------------------------------------
A: The try/except in fetch_real_shopping_data catches any Exception from
GoogleSearch() or .get_dict(). It prints the error to console (visible in
Vercel logs) and returns an empty list [].

Back in index.py:
    results = fetch_real_shopping_data(query, location)
    if not results:
        return jsonify({"error": "No matching product found or rate limit blocked"}), 404

The frontend then shows the error message to the user.

Q24. What are the mock services and why do they exist?
-------------------------------------------------------
A: bigbasket_service.py, blinkit_service.py, and instamart_service.py are
scaffolding for future platform-specific scrapers. They use asyncio.sleep() to
simulate realistic scraping delays (0.5-2.2 seconds) and return mock data from
normalizer.py's generate_mock_data().

They're NOT currently called from index.py — all data comes from SerpAPI.
I built them as architecture for a planned Phase 2: direct Playwright scraping
of each grocery platform. The idea was to run all 3 as concurrent async tasks
(asyncio.gather), which would be faster than sequential requests.

I kept them in the codebase as documentation of the intended architecture.

Q25. What is SerpAPI and why use it instead of scraping Google directly?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
A: SerpAPI is a paid API that provides Google Search data in structured JSON.
Scraping Google directly is:
  1. Against Google's ToS
  2. Technically challenging - Google uses CAPTCHAs, IP blocking, JavaScript-
     rendered content, and constantly changing DOM structure
  3. Unreliable - would break frequently as Google updates its HTML

SerpAPI handles all of that as a managed service. I send a simple API request,
it handles the scraping, and I get clean JSON back. The tradeoff is cost
(100 free searches/month, paid tiers for more).

Q26. What parameters do you send to SerpAPI and what do they do?
-----------------------------------------------------------------
A:
  engine: "google_shopping"  - Tells SerpAPI to use Google's shopping parser
  q: <user's query>          - The search term
  api_key: SERPAPI_KEY        - Authentication
  gl: "in"                   - Geolocation country code = India
                               Changes what regional Google results you see
  hl: "en"                   - Host language = English
  location: <user's location> - Optional city/zip for localized pricing
                               e.g., "Mumbai" shows Mumbai-relevant sellers

Q27. How is the API key protected?
------------------------------------
A: The key is stored in a .env file which is listed in .gitignore so it's never
committed to GitHub. When deployed to Vercel, the key is set as an Environment
Variable in the Vercel dashboard (accessible as a secure secret, not visible
in the code repository). In the Python code, os.getenv("SERPAPI_KEY") reads it
at runtime. I had one incident where I accidentally committed the key (visible
in git history as "chore: remove leaked serpapi key") — I immediately rotated
the key and removed it from the code.

SECTION 5: ARCHITECTURE & DESIGN DECISIONS
================================================================================

Q29. Why Flask over Django or FastAPI?
----------------------------------------
A: Flask fits this project perfectly:
  - Only 3 routes needed — Django's ORM, admin, auth, forms are all overkill
  - Flask is lightweight (~10MB vs Django's heavier footprint)
  - FastAPI would be ideal if I needed async routes and auto-generated docs,
    but SerpAPI's Python client is synchronous, so async wouldn't help here
  - Flask has excellent Vercel serverless support (@vercel/python)

I'd consider FastAPI if I later add WebSockets for live price updates, or if I
switch to a fully async architecture with httpx + concurrent platform scrapers.

Q30. Why no database?
-----------------------
A: This version of BudgetMart is a pure real-time query tool. Every search hits
the live API — there's no need to persist data because prices change constantly.
Adding a database would introduce:
  - Schema design and migrations
  - Connection management
  - Infrastructure cost
  - Stale data risk (cached prices that are hours old)

The tradeoff is that you can't track price history or trends. A future version
with historical charting would absolutely need a database (PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB
for time-series data would be ideal).

Q31. What are the tradeoffs of using Vercel serverless vs a traditional server?
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A:

Advantages of Vercel Serverless:
  + Free hosting with no credit card required
  + Auto-scaling (handles traffic spikes automatically)
  + Zero DevOps (no server management, no uptime monitoring needed)
  + Global CDN for frontend
  + Automatic HTTPS
  + GitHub integration (auto-deploy on push)

Disadvantages for this project:
  - Cold starts: First request after idle takes 2-3 seconds extra
  - Stateless: In-memory cache (SimpleCache) is wiped on cold start
  - Execution time limit: 10 seconds max per function (fine for SerpAPI)
  - No persistent connections (can't keep a DB connection pool warm)
  - Vendor lock-in

For BudgetMart's use case (low-medium traffic, simplicity priority), the
tradeoffs strongly favor Vercel.

Q32. How would you scale this application for 100x more traffic?
-----------------------------------------------------------------
A: Several changes:
  1. Replace SimpleCache with Redis (Upstash Redis for serverless-friendly Redis)
     - Persistent across cold starts
     - Shared across multiple function instances

  2. Add rate limiting to the /search endpoint
     - Prevent single users from exhausting SerpAPI quota
     - Use Redis for rate limit counters

  3. Upgrade SerpAPI plan or implement request queuing
     - SerpAPI limits requests per hour on paid plans

  4. Consider a database for result caching beyond 5 minutes
     - Store query → results mapping with longer TTL
     - Reduces SerpAPI calls significantly for popular queries

  5. Move to a persistent server (Render, Railway, AWS EC2) if cold starts
     become a UX problem

  6. Add monitoring (Sentry for errors, Grafana for metrics)

Q33. Why is the caching not very effective on Vercel?
------------------------------------------------------
A: Flask-Caching SimpleCache stores data in Python process memory (a dict).
Vercel's serverless functions are stateless — each cold start creates a brand
new Python process with an empty dict. Even if the same function instance is
reused (warm invocations), if Vercel spins up a new instance to handle concurrent
requests, they each have separate empty caches.

For caching to actually work across all instances and persist across cold starts,
you need external cache storage. The solution is Redis:
  cache = Cache(app, config={
      'CACHE_TYPE': 'RedisCache',
      'CACHE_REDIS_URL': os.getenv('REDIS_URL')
  })

Upstash offers serverless Redis with a free tier perfect for this use case.

Q34. Walk me through how the backend is deployed on Vercel.
------------------------------------------------------------
A:
  1. The backend/ folder is linked as a Vercel project via GitHub
  2. vercel.json configures:
       builds: [{"src": "api/index.py", "use": "@vercel/python"}]
       routes: [{"src": "/(.*)", "dest": "api/index.py"}]
  3. @vercel/python runtime:
       - Installs packages from requirements.txt
       - Compiles api/index.py as a serverless function entry point
       - Wraps the Flask WSGI app in Vercel's function handler
  4. Routes config forwards ALL request paths to the Flask app
     (Flask then handles routing with @app.route decorators)
  5. SERPAPI_KEY is set as a secret Environment Variable in Vercel dashboard
  6. Each git push to main triggers a new Vercel deployment

Q35. What was the "Fix Vercel WSGI async crash" commit about?
--------------------------------------------------------------
A: Early in development, I had an issue where the Flask app would crash on Vercel
with async-related errors. The root cause was that I was trying to use asyncio
inside Flask routes without proper async support. Vercel's Python runtime uses
WSGI (synchronous), not ASGI (asynchronous). The fix was to ensure all route
handlers are synchronous functions and that the async mock services (which used
asyncio.sleep) were not being called from the synchronous Flask routes.
I moved async code entirely out of the request path.

Q36. What was the "Copy requirements.txt to api directory" fix?
---------------------------------------------------------------
A: Vercel's @vercel/python runtime looks for requirements.txt relative to the
entry point file (api/index.py). My original requirements.txt was in backend/
(parent directory), not in backend/api/. Vercel couldn't find it, so the
deployment was missing dependencies (serpapi wasn't installed, causing ImportError).
The fix was to place a requirements.txt copy in api/ directory alongside index.py.

SECTION 7: PYTHON/PROGRAMMING CONCEPTS
================================================================================

Q37. Explain how python-dotenv works.
--------------------------------------
A: python-dotenv reads a .env file and loads its key=value pairs as environment
variables into os.environ.

In serp_service.py:
    from dotenv import load_dotenv
    load_dotenv()
    API_KEY = os.getenv("SERPAPI_KEY", "")

load_dotenv() searches for a .env file starting from the current directory
(walks up the directory tree). os.getenv("SERPAPI_KEY") then reads it from
os.environ. The second argument "" is the default if the key isn't found.

This keeps secrets out of source code. The .gitignore excludes .env files.

Q38. What is asyncio and why is it in the mock services?
---------------------------------------------------------
A: asyncio is Python's built-in library for asynchronous/concurrent programming
using an event loop. Instead of blocking while waiting for I/O (like a network
request), async functions can yield control back to the event loop, which can
run other tasks.

In the mock services:
    async def fetch_bigbasket_data(...):
        await asyncio.sleep(random.uniform(0.8, 2.2))  # simulate network delay
        ...

The await asyncio.sleep() simulates the I/O wait of real HTTP requests without
actually blocking. The intent was to run all 3 services concurrently:
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch_bigbasket_data(query, normalized),
        fetch_blinkit_data(query, normalized),
        fetch_instamart_data(query, normalized)
    )

This would give ~2.2s total (slowest single service) instead of 0.8+1.5+1.8=4.1s
for sequential calls.

Q39. What is the services/__init__.py file for?
------------------------------------------------
A: It makes the services/ directory a Python package. Without __init__.py,
Python wouldn't recognize services/ as a package, and you couldn't do:
    from api.services.serp_service import fetch_real_shopping_data

In Python 3.3+ (namespace packages), __init__.py is technically optional for
simple cases, but it's still considered best practice to include it explicitly.

Q40. Explain list comprehensions and how you use them.
-------------------------------------------------------
A: List comprehensions create a new list by applying an expression to each item
in an iterable, with optional filtering.

In renderResults() (JS equivalent logic, but here's the Python pattern):
    inStockItems = [r for r in sortedResults if r['stock'] and r['price'] is not None]
    prices = [item['price'] for item in inStockItems]
    lowestPrice = min(prices) if prices else float('inf')

This is more readable and faster than equivalent for loops for simple transforms.

Q41. What bugs did you encounter and how did you fix them?
-----------------------------------------------------------
A: Several notable issues:

  1. CORS Error on Frontend:
     Problem: Browser blocked fetch() requests from frontend to Flask backend
     with "No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header" error.
     Fix: Added Flask-CORS to the backend: CORS(app)

  2. SerpAPI key leaked in git:
     Problem: Accidentally committed the .env file (had SERPAPI_KEY in plain text)
     Fix: Added .env to .gitignore, deleted key from SerpAPI dashboard,
     generated a new key, never committed again.
     Lesson: Always check .gitignore BEFORE first commit.

  3. Vercel deployment missing dependencies:
     Problem: Vercel couldn't install packages — requirements.txt was in backend/
     but Vercel looked for it next to api/index.py
     Fix: Copied requirements.txt into backend/api/ directory

  4. Async/WSGI mismatch crash:
     Problem: Flask routes were trying to use asyncio which conflicted with
     Vercel's WSGI runner. The app would crash with RuntimeError about event loops.
     Fix: Made all Flask routes fully synchronous. Kept async code only in the
     standalone mock services (which are not currently called).

  5. OOS items showing up at the top of price sort:
     Problem: Out-of-stock items have price = null. null compared with < in
     JavaScript sorts null as 0 (falsely appearing cheapest).
     Fix: In sort comparator, explicitly check stock status first, push OOS items
     to the end regardless of sort mode.

Q44. How would you add direct platform scraping (replacing SerpAPI)?
---------------------------------------------------------------------
A: The mock services in the codebase show the intended architecture.
Real implementation would:
  1. Use Playwright (Python) for each platform
     - BigBasket: search URL pattern is known, CSS selectors for price/title
     - Blinkit: similar DOM-based extraction
     - Instamart: requires geolocation header for correct city pricing

  2. Run them concurrently:
       results = await asyncio.gather(
           fetch_bigbasket_data(query),
           fetch_blinkit_data(query),
           fetch_instamart_data(query)
       )

  3. Switch Flask to ASGI (FastAPI or Quart) to support true async routes

  4. Challenges:
       - Anti-bot measures (Cloudflare, CAPTCHAs)
       - Dynamic pricing (prices change between search and page load)
       - Login-gated content (some prices require account login)
       - Deployment: Playwright requires browser binaries (~300MB) — doesn't fit
         Vercel's serverless limits; would need Railway or a Docker container

Q45. What security considerations does this app have?
------------------------------------------------------
A: Current security measures:
  - API keys stored in environment variables (not code)
  - .gitignore prevents .env from being committed
  - CORS configured (though currently allows all origins with *)

Security issues to address for production:
  1. Rate limiting: Anyone can spam /search and exhaust the SerpAPI quota.
     Solution: Flask-Limiter (e.g., 10 requests/minute per IP)

  2. Input validation: While Flask sanitizes URL params, I should validate
     query length (max 100 chars) and sanitize for any injection attempts

  3. CORS restriction: Change from CORS(app) (allows all origins) to
     CORS(app, origins=["https://budgetmart.vercel.app"])

  4. HTTPS: Vercel enforces HTTPS automatically — covered

  5. SerpAPI key exposure: Only in .env and Vercel env vars — covered

  6. XSS: Currently I set innerHTML with user-influenced content (product names
     from SerpAPI). Malicious content in a product title could inject script tags.
     Fix: Sanitize the product_name field before injecting into innerHTML, or
     use textContent instead of innerHTML for user-data fields.

Q46. How would you add user authentication to BudgetMart?
----------------------------------------------------------
A: For a simple wishlist/alert system:
  1. Backend: Add Flask-JWT-Extended for token-based auth
  2. Database: Users table (email, password hash with bcrypt)
  3. Endpoints: POST /auth/register, POST /auth/login → returns JWT
  4. Protected endpoints: POST /wishlist, GET /wishlist (require Authorization header)
  5. Frontend: Store JWT in localStorage or httpOnly cookie
     (httpOnly cookie is more secure — prevents XSS token theft)

  Simpler alternative: Use Auth0 or Supabase Auth (managed auth services)
  which handle token management, OAuth providers, and session security.

Q47. What would you do differently if rebuilding this today?
------------------------------------------------------------
A:
  1. Use Redis from the start instead of SimpleCache (stateless cache is useless
     on serverless)

  2. Add rate limiting immediately (SerpAPI quota protection)

  3. Consider Next.js instead of plain HTML+JS for better developer experience
     while keeping it lightweight (Next.js API routes could replace Flask)

  4. Use TypeScript instead of plain JS to catch type errors at compile time

  5. Add monitoring (Sentry.io) from day 1 to capture errors in production

  6. Set up proper CI/CD pipeline with tests before deploying

  7. Restrict CORS to specific origins instead of wildcard

  8. Use a CDN for product images (proxy through backend to avoid mixed content
     issues) rather than directly embedding third-party image URLs

================================================================================
SECTION 10: QUICK-FIRE QUESTIONS
================================================================================

Q48. What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous programming?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A: Synchronous: code executes line by line. Each operation must complete before
the next one starts. Blocking I/O (network requests) pauses the entire program.

Asynchronous: I/O operations are started and the program continues executing
other tasks while waiting for the I/O to complete. Python's asyncio uses an event
loop to manage this. async/await is the syntax. You "await" an operation, meaning
"start this and come back when it's done."

Example: Fetching 3 URLs:
  - Sync: fetch1 (2s) → fetch2 (1.5s) → fetch3 (2.2s) = 5.7s total
  - Async: all three start simultaneously = 2.2s total (slowest one)

Q49. What does @app.route() do in Flask?
-----------------------------------------
A: It's a decorator that registers a view function to handle HTTP requests for
a specific URL path. When Flask receives a request for "/search", it looks up
the registered functions and calls the one mapped to that path.

Under the hood, Flask maintains a URL map (Werkzeug's routing system). The
decorator adds a rule: ("GET", "/search") → search function.

Q50. What is the difference between GET and POST requests?
-----------------------------------------------------------
A: GET: Retrieves data. Parameters are in the URL (query string). Idempotent
(calling it multiple times has the same effect). Can be bookmarked/cached.
BudgetMart uses GET for /search because:
  - It's a read operation (fetching prices, not modifying anything)
  - URL-based params make it cacheable (Flask-Caching uses URL as key)
  - Can be easily tested by pasting URL in browser

POST: Sends data to create/modify something. Parameters are in the request body.
Not idempotent (submitting twice could create duplicate records).
Would use POST for: creating a user account, adding to wishlist, etc.

Q51. What is a serverless function?
-------------------------------------
A: A serverless function is a unit of code that runs in response to HTTP requests
without a persistent server. The cloud provider (Vercel, AWS Lambda) manages:
  - Server provisioning
  - Scaling (automatically handle more traffic)
  - OS and runtime maintenance

Each request potentially runs in a fresh isolated environment (cold start).
Between requests, no resources are consumed (and no costs incurred).

"Serverless" is a misnomer — there are still servers, but you don't manage them.

Q52. What is JSON and how is it used in this project?
------------------------------------------------------
A: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data format for
human-readable data exchange. It uses key-value pairs (objects) and arrays.

In BudgetMart:
  - Backend → Frontend: The /search endpoint returns JSON arrays with product data
  - Frontend reads it with response.json() (returns JavaScript objects/arrays)
  - Python Flask uses jsonify() to serialize Python dicts/lists to JSON strings
    and set Content-Type: application/json header

Q53. What is a Python virtual environment?
-------------------------------------------
A: A virtual environment (.venv) is an isolated Python installation with its own
site-packages directory. This lets you install packages (Flask, serpapi, etc.)
for this project without affecting the system Python or other projects.

Commands:
  python -m venv .venv           # create virtual env
  .venv\Scripts\activate          # activate (Windows)
  pip install -r requirements.txt # install project dependencies

This is why .venv/ is in .gitignore — it's large (hundreds of MB) and can
be recreated from requirements.txt on any machine.

================================================================================
SECTION 11: BEHAVIORAL QUESTIONS (PROJECT-RELATED)
================================================================================

Q54. What was the most challenging part of building BudgetMart?
----------------------------------------------------------------
A: The most challenging part was getting the Vercel deployment to work correctly.
I ran into three separate issues:
  1. The requirements.txt was in the wrong directory for Vercel's Python runtime
  2. The asyncio in mock services conflicted with WSGI's synchronous nature
  3. An API key was accidentally committed to git

Each required investigating Vercel's build logs, reading documentation, and
understanding how serverless Python differs from traditional Flask deployment.
The debugging process taught me a lot about serverless architecture.

Q55. What did you learn building this project?
-----------------------------------------------
A:
  1. Full-stack integration: How frontend fetch() connects to backend routes,
     why CORS exists, and how JSON flows between layers.

  2. Third-party API integration: Reading documentation, handling rate limits,
     normalizing inconsistent response formats.

  3. Regex: Writing complex patterns for quantity extraction gave me confidence
     with regex — understanding character classes, groups, quantifiers, boundaries.

  4. Security awareness: The accidental key leak taught me to be vigilant about
     .gitignore and never hardcode secrets.

  5. Serverless limitations: That "serverless" doesn't mean "stateless problems
     disappear" — in-memory state truly doesn't persist.

  6. CSS animations: Glassmorphism, keyframe animations, and CSS custom properties
     for maintaining a consistent design system.

Q56. How would you present this project to a non-technical person?
-------------------------------------------------------------------
A: "Imagine you want to buy a bottle of Amul milk. Instead of opening BigBasket,
then Blinkit, then Instamart separately to compare prices, you just type 'Amul Milk'
in BudgetMart and instantly see all prices side by side. The cheapest one gets
highlighted in green. You can then click a button to go directly to that store
and buy it. It's like Google Shopping but focused specifically on Indian grocery
delivery apps."

================================================================================
                         END OF INTERVIEW QUESTIONS
================================================================================

QUICK REFERENCE - KEY NUMBERS TO REMEMBER:
  - Cache TTL: 5 minutes (300 seconds)
  - Max results fetched: 12
  - SerpAPI free tier: 100 searches/month
  - Stagger animation delay: 0.1 seconds per card
  - Mock service delays: 0.5 - 2.2 seconds (simulate scraping)
  - Flask version: 3.0.2
  - Backend port (local): 5000

KEY DESIGN DECISIONS TO ARTICULATE:
  - No framework (Vanilla JS) → simplicity + learning
  - SerpAPI over scraping → reliability + legal
  - Flask over Django/FastAPI → minimal routes + sync API
  - Vercel over Render → free tier, no credit card
  - SimpleCache over Redis → simplicity (aware of serverless limitation)
  - No database → real-time pricing doesn't benefit from persistence
================================================================================
